target.title: fix(feishu): route SDK HTTP through ambient proxy
target.body:
AI-assisted contribution.

## Summary

- Route Feishu/Lark SDK HTTP requests through OpenClaw's ambient proxy agent and set `proxy: false` so axios does not take a separate HTTPS proxy path.
- Reuse the same proxy-aware HTTP instance for `WSClient` bootstrap requests while preserving WebSocket heartbeat config and callbacks.
- Preserve request-level `httpAgent`/`httpsAgent` values for ordinary ambient proxy env, but override them while OpenClaw managed proxy mode is active so Feishu SDK HTTP cannot bypass the operator proxy boundary.

Fixes #48949

## Verification

- `pnpm install`
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/plugin-sdk/extension-shared.test.ts`
- `pnpm format:check extensions/feishu/src/client.ts extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test:extension feishu`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `codex review --base origin/main` (twice before the managed-proxy follow-up; no actionable regressions found)

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Feishu token/API and WebSocket bootstrap failed when the gateway process had `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` configured, producing `tenant_access_token` failures and 400 responses containing "The plain HTTP request was sent to HTTPS port".

Real environment tested: WSL2 Ubuntu host running OpenClaw 2026.5.22 with the systemd user gateway and `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY`/`OPENCLAW_PROXY_URL=http://127.0.0.1:7890`. To avoid replacing this source checkout into my live gateway, I first applied the equivalent dist hotfix to the installed Feishu plugin, restarted the gateway, then ported the same fix to source in this PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Restarted the installed OpenClaw gateway with the equivalent Feishu dist hotfix, then ran `systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway`, `openclaw channels status --channel feishu --deep --probe`, and a filtered `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` check for both prior failure strings and successful Feishu startup lines.

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output and runtime log excerpt from the live OpenClaw gateway after the fix:

```console
$ systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway
active

$ openclaw channels status --channel feishu --deep --probe
Checking channel status (probe)...
Gateway reachable.
- Feishu default: enabled, configured, running, works

Tip: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli#status adds gateway health probes to status output (requires a reachable gateway).

$ journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since '2026-05-25 15:12:04' --no-pager | rg 'Request failed with status code 400|connect failed|unable to connect|socket hang up|tenant_access_token|bot identity|ws client ready|bot open_id resolved'
May 25 15:12:04 jamietop node[13765]: 2026-05-25T15:12:04.501+08:00 [feishu] feishu[default]: bot open_id resolved: ou_[redacted]
May 25 15:12:04 jamietop node[13765]: 2026-05-25T15:12:04.945+08:00 [info]: [ '[ws]', 'ws client ready' ]
```

The probe reported `Feishu default: enabled, configured, running, works`; gateway logs showed `bot open_id resolved` and `[ws] ws client ready`. The log grep included the prior failure strings (`Request failed with status code 400`, `connect failed`, `unable to connect`, `socket hang up`, and `tenant_access_token`) and returned only the two successful startup lines after the final restart.

Observed result after fix: Feishu API probing and WebSocket startup both recovered in the proxy-enabled environment.

What was not tested: I did not swap this source checkout into the live gateway or run the repository-wide `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`; this PR includes targeted source tests, `test:extension feishu`, `check:changed`, managed-proxy agent override unit coverage, and live validation through the equivalent installed-package hotfix.

5. Git Operations

All verification steps passed.

PR Creation
-----------
GitHub CLI auth token is invalid. To create PR manually:

1. Go to: https://github.com/zhangguiping-xydt/openclaw/compare/feat/issue-86230
2. Ensure base is `openclaw:main` and compare is `feat/issue-86230`
3. Use the following PR body:

Fixes #86230

**Problem**
`openclaw cron add --at` help text advertises `+duration` (e.g., `+30m`) as valid, but the validator rejected it with:
`Error: Invalid --at. Use an ISO timestamp or a duration like 20m.`

Modified `parseAt()` in `src/cli/cron-cli/shared.ts` to strip the optional leading `+` before calling `parseDurationMs()`. This minimal change makes the validator accept `+duration` while preserving backward compatibility for bare durations (`30m`).

- Integration test covers end-to-end CLI behavior
- Runtime simulation verified all cases pass
- Type-checking successful
- No other components affected

**Backward Compatibility**
Fully maintained. Existing usage with bare durations (`30m`) continues to work.

target.title: fix(memory-core): filter REM dreaming candidates to light-staged entries
target.body:
## Summary

REM dreaming re-ingested the full short-term recall store independently, ignoring which entries had actually been staged by light sleep. The confidence formula weights accumulated `averageScore` and `recallStrength`, so older high-recall entries could dominate freshly staged candidates and break the intended light-to-REM-to-deep pipeline.

## Root cause

`runRemDreaming()` read all short-term recall entries in the lookback window and passed them unfiltered to `previewRemDreaming()`. REM candidate selection then ranked by accumulated recall history rather than by the current light-sleep staging signal.

## Fix

1. `short-term-promotion.ts`: add staged-key reading that only returns light-staged keys still pending REM consideration.
2. `dreaming-phases.ts`: prefer live light-staged entries for REM, fall back to the full recent store when staged keys are stale or absent, and mark staged entries as REM-considered without giving every considered entry promotion credit.
3. Regression tests cover pending staged keys, consumed staged keys, and the existing REM/promotion behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: REM dreaming now synthesizes from the current live light-staged recall entries instead of reselecting unrelated older high-recall entries from the full short-term store.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, rebased on current `origin/main`, using the memory-core dreaming and short-term promotion implementation with real temp-workspace stores.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the local OpenClaw run:

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/steipete/Projects/clawdbot3

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  97 passed (97)
```

Observed result after fix: staged light keys are consumed by REM consideration, stale/non-live staged keys no longer block REM from falling back to recent recalls, and the focused memory-core regression suite stays green.

What was not tested: a multi-day live memory-core deployment with production user memory was not replayed for this PR.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts"`

Closes #86249

target.title: fix(telegram): propagate forum topic names into agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Forum topic names are tracked in the Telegram topic-name cache from `forum_topic_created` / `forum_topic_edited` service events, but two runtime paths missed that cache.

`bot-native-commands.ts` built agent context with `IsForum: true` without resolving the cached topic name, so agents could not tell which forum topic they were handling. `action-runtime.ts` created and edited forum topics without persisting the resulting topic metadata back into the same account-scoped cache, so newly created or renamed topics stayed unnamed until a later service event.

## Changes

1. `bot-native-commands.ts`: resolve the account-scoped topic cache and include `TopicName` in agent context when available.
2. `action-runtime.ts`: after successful `createForumTopic` / `editForumTopic`, write the returned topic name and metadata into the account-scoped topic cache.
3. Regression tests cover native command context lookup plus create/edit action cache writes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram forum topic names created or edited through OpenClaw are now available to later Telegram context construction through the account-scoped topic cache.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, rebased on current `origin/main`, using the Telegram runtime code path with deterministic Telegram API stubs and the real topic-name cache store wiring.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/topic-name-cache.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.dm-threads.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the local OpenClaw run:

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/steipete/Projects/clawdbot3

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  119 passed (119)
```

Observed result after fix: the create/edit action tests read back the created or renamed topic from the account-scoped topic cache, and the native-command context test emits the cached `TopicName` for forum messages.

What was not tested: a live Telegram Bot API create/edit forum-topic call against a real supergroup was not run for this PR.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/topic-name-cache.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.dm-threads.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/topic-name-cache.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.dm-threads.test.ts"`

Closes #86024

target.title: fix: use context-aware reserveTokensFloor in overflow recovery hint
target.body:
## Summary

The overflow recovery hint always suggests setting `reserveTokensFloor` to 20000, regardless of the model's context window size. For models with larger context (e.g., 200K+), this suggestion is too conservative and leads to repeated overflow resets.

This PR makes the hint context-aware by computing a proportional reserve token floor based on the model's actual context window:

| Context window | Suggested reserveTokensFloor |
|---|---|
| >= 1,000,000 | 100,000 |
| >= 200,000 | 50,000 |
| >= 100,000 | 35,000 |
| < 100,000 or unknown | 20,000 (default) |

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix (better UX for overflow recovery)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Overflow recovery hint now reflects the model's actual context window instead of hardcoding 20000.

Real environment tested: Linux (x86_64), Node 22.22, local OpenClaw checkout.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx -e "import { computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor } from './src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.js'; console.log('1M+:', computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor(1_000_000)); console.log('200K+:', computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor(200_000)); console.log('100K+:', computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor(100_000)); console.log('65K:', computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor(65_000)); console.log('undefined:', computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor(undefined));"
```

Evidence after fix (terminal output):

```
$ node --import tsx -e "import { computeContextAwareReserveTokensFloor } from './src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.js'; ...
1M+: 100000
200K+: 50000
100K+: 35000
65K: 20000
undefined: 20000
```

Observed result after fix: The function returns proportional values matching the context window size thresholds.

What was not tested: Live overflow scenario (requires real context limit hit). Unit tests in the same file cover the integration.

## Security Impact

None. Only error message text is changed.

- `codex review --base origin/main` (twice before the managed-proxy follow-up; no actionable regressions found)

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Feishu token/API and WebSocket bootstrap failed when the gateway process had `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` configured, producing `tenant_access_token` failures and 400 responses containing "The plain HTTP request was sent to HTTPS port".

Real environment tested: WSL2 Ubuntu host running OpenClaw 2026.5.22 with the systemd user gateway and `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY`/`OPENCLAW_PROXY_URL=http://127.0.0.1:7890`. To avoid replacing this source checkout into my live gateway, I first applied the equivalent dist hotfix to the installed Feishu plugin, restarted the gateway, then ported the same fix to source in this PR.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Restarted the installed OpenClaw gateway with the equivalent Feishu dist hotfix, then ran `systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway`, `openclaw channels status --channel feishu --deep --probe`, and a filtered `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` check for both prior failure strings and successful Feishu startup lines.

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output and runtime log excerpt from the live OpenClaw gateway after the fix:

```console
$ systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway
active

$ openclaw channels status --channel feishu --deep --probe
Checking channel status (probe)...
Gateway reachable.
- Feishu default: enabled, configured, running, works

Tip: https://docs.openclaw.ai/cli#status adds gateway health probes to status output (requires a reachable gateway).

$ journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since '2026-05-25 15:12:04' --no-pager | rg 'Request failed with status code 400|connect failed|unable to connect|socket hang up|tenant_access_token|bot identity|ws client ready|bot open_id resolved'
May 25 15:12:04 jamietop node[13765]: 2026-05-25T15:12:04.501+08:00 [feishu] feishu[default]: bot open_id resolved: ou_[redacted]
May 25 15:12:04 jamietop node[13765]: 2026-05-25T15:12:04.945+08:00 [info]: [ '[ws]', 'ws client ready' ]
```

The probe reported `Feishu default: enabled, configured, running, works`; gateway logs showed `bot open_id resolved` and `[ws] ws client ready`. The log grep included the prior failure strings (`Request failed with status code 400`, `connect failed`, `unable to connect`, `socket hang up`, and `tenant_access_token`) and returned only the two successful startup lines after the final restart.

Observed result after fix: Feishu API probing and WebSocket startup both recovered in the proxy-enabled environment.

What was not tested: I did not swap this source checkout into the live gateway or run the repository-wide `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`; this PR includes targeted source tests, `test:extension feishu`, `check:changed`, managed-proxy agent override unit coverage, and live validation through the equivalent installed-package hotfix.

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(plugins): scope startup metadata manifest reads
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway startup could still rebuild plugin metadata from every installed plugin manifest before narrowing to the configured startup set.
- Solution: carry a conservative startup plugin-id scope into metadata snapshot loading when the Installed Plugin Index can prove the narrowed set safely.
- What changed: metadata snapshots can carry scoped `pluginIds`; lookup/startup paths pass a conservative `pluginIdScope`; startup scoping carries indexed `activation.onConfigPaths` owners such as `browser`; persisted installed indexes preserve that `startup.configPaths` metadata; scoped current snapshots require an exact caller-requested scope before reuse; unscoped Gateway config-validation snapshots are not reused for restrictive startup bootstrap.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no plugin config shape changes, no provider/channel activation semantics changes, no compat bundled-discovery shortcut.

## Motivation

#70533 is a startup stability problem: a restrictive plugin setup should not crash-loop or pay full manifest reconstruction cost just because unrelated installed plugins exist. This keeps the startup path on the Installed Plugin Index when it can safely prove the manifest subset, and falls back to the old unscoped behavior when it cannot.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #70533
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: restrictive gateway startup plugin scope still caused full installed-index manifest reconstruction before startup narrowing.
- Real environment tested: isolated Linux testserver disposable repo, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2, `origin/main` at `a13468320c` compared with this patch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran TSX diagnostic scripts through the real `loadPluginLookUpTable -> loadPluginMetadataSnapshot -> loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex -> resolveGatewayStartupPluginPlanFromRegistry` path using valid 96-plugin Installed Plugin Index fixtures.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "label": "after-rebased-patch-confirm",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 1,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "loadedPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the Installed Plugin Index still contains 96 plugins, but metadata manifest reconstruction reads only the one allowed startup plugin manifest; startup selection remains `["openai"]`.
- Additional blocker-fix proof:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "browser": { "enabled": true },
    "channels": {},
    "plugins": {
      "allow": ["openai"],
      "slots": { "memory": "none" }
    }
  },
  "results": [
    {
      "label": "indexed-config-path-scope",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 2,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "loadedPluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    },
    {
      "label": "persisted-index-config-path-roundtrip",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 2,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "loadedPluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
      "persistedBrowserConfigPaths": ["browser"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    },
    {
      "label": "legacy-index-safe-fallback",
      "indexPluginCount": 96,
      "manifestPluginCount": 96,
      "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"],
      "diagnosticCount": 0
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed blocker-fix result: `browser.enabled=true` is preserved under a restrictive `plugins.allow=["openai"]`; new and persisted indexes keep `startup.configPaths=["browser"]` and stay scoped to 2 manifests, while legacy indexes without indexed config-path metadata fall back unscoped instead of losing `browser`.
- Full Gateway startup path proof:

```json
{
  "path": [
    "readConfigFileSnapshotWithPluginMetadata",
    "loadGatewayStartupConfigSnapshot",
    "prepareGatewayPluginBootstrap"
  ],
  "validationSnapshot": {
    "pluginIds": "unscoped",
    "manifestPluginCount": 96
  },
  "bootstrapManifestLoads": [["browser"], ["browser"], ["browser", "openai"]],
  "bootstrapLookup": {
    "pluginIds": ["browser", "openai"],
    "manifestPluginCount": 2
  },
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai", "browser"]
}
```

- Observed full-path result: the real config-validation snapshot is still unscoped with 96 manifests, but startup bootstrap does not reuse it for the restrictive allowlist. The final bootstrap lookup reloads the scoped `["browser","openai"]` manifests and starts the expected plugins.
- What was not tested: no production credentials, no persistent services, no real external provider/channel account.
- Before evidence:

```json
{
  "label": "before-rebased-origin-main",
  "indexPluginCount": 96,
  "manifestPluginCount": 96,
  "startupPluginIds": ["openai"],
  "diagnosticCount": 0
}
```

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: metadata snapshot loading had no way to receive a conservative startup plugin-id scope after the Installed Plugin Index was available, so it reconstructed the installed manifest registry for the full index before startup planning narrowed plugin ids.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered startup narrowing and metadata memoization separately, but not the manifest reconstruction count for restrictive gateway startup allowlists.
- Contributing context (if known): some startup intent, config-path activation, channel, provider, slot, and harness decisions can only be trusted when the Installed Plugin Index can map them without reading manifests; unsafe cases need to stay unscoped.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/commands/status.summary.redaction.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/pr84628-startup-snapshot-proof.temp.test.ts` (temporary proof test, not committed)
  - `src/plugins/plugin-lookup-table.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/installed-plugin-index-store.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: restrictive `plugins.allow` startup config passes a narrow scope to installed-index manifest reconstruction; config-path activation owners such as `browser` stay included; conservative fallback remains unscoped for compat discovery, legacy indexes, or unmappable providers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it asserts the actual manifest-registry load boundary without needing external plugin credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway startup with a restrictive plugin allowlist can avoid reading unrelated installed plugin manifests when the startup scope is safe to prove from the Installed Plugin Index. Unsafe configs keep existing unscoped behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> read 96 manifests -> startup plan -> openai

After:
Installed Index (96 plugins) -> conservative scope ["browser","openai"] when browser config is present -> read 2 manifests -> startup plan -> openai + browser
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux testserver disposable run
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.2
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): synthetic installed plugin fixture; no external channel account
- Relevant config (redacted): `plugins.allow=["openai"]`, `plugins.slots.memory="none"`; blocker regression also uses `browser.enabled=true`

### Steps

1. On `origin/main`, run the diagnostic fixture through `loadPluginLookUpTable`.
2. Apply this patch.
3. Run the same diagnostic fixture again.
4. Run focused regression tests, format, typecheck, and targeted lint.

### Expected

- Startup still selects `openai`.
- `browser.enabled=true` still selects `browser` even when `plugins.allow` only lists `openai`.
- Manifest reconstruction only reads the scoped startup plugin(s) after the patch.
- Conservative fallback paths remain unscoped when the Installed Plugin Index cannot prove the mapping.

### Actual

- Before: `manifestPluginCount=96`, `startupPluginIds=["openai"]`.
- After: `manifestPluginCount=1`, `loadedPluginIds=["openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai"]`.
- After blocker repair: `manifestPluginCount=2`, `loadedPluginIds=["browser","openai"]`, `startupPluginIds=["openai","browser"]` for `browser.enabled=true`.
- After persisted-index repair: persisted read/write roundtrip keeps `persistedBrowserConfigPaths=["browser"]`, `manifestPluginCount=2`, and `diagnosticCount=0`.
- After Gateway startup snapshot repair: config-validation snapshot remains `pluginIds="unscoped"` with `manifestPluginCount=96`, but bootstrap reloads scoped `["browser","openai"]` and lookup ends at `manifestPluginCount=2`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

target.title: fix(slack): call markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle in reply pipeline
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #84049.

Slack's reply dispatch pipeline was missing `markRunComplete()` calls, causing the typing/status indicator to remain visible after reply delivery until the fallback TTL expired (~2 minutes), instead of clearing on `doneHoldMs`.

## Root Cause

Core typing cleanup requires **both** `run-complete` and `dispatch-idle` state before prompt cleanup. The Slack dispatch path only called `markDispatchIdle()` — never `markRunComplete()` — so the first half of that condition was never satisfied.

Discord and the shared message handler paths already call both markers in the correct order. This PR brings Slack into parity.

## Changes

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts`**
- Destructure `markRunComplete` from `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping` result
- Call `markRunComplete()` before `markDispatchIdle()` in the normal `finally` path
- Call `markRunComplete()` before `markDispatchIdle()` in the `onPreDispatchFailure` callback

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.preview-fallback.test.ts`**
- Add `markRunComplete: () => {}` to the `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping` mock so existing tests continue to work

**`extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.lifecycle.test.ts`** (new)
- Tests that `markRunComplete` is called before `markDispatchIdle` on normal dispatch completion
- Tests that `markRunComplete` is called before `markDispatchIdle` when `onPreDispatchFailure` fires
- Tests that both are called exactly once on successful dispatch

## Real behavior proof

### Before the fix

Slack typing indicator persisted for ~2 minutes after reply delivery. The root cause was verified in `dispatch.ts`: `markRunComplete` was never destructured or called from `createReplyDispatcherWithTyping`'s result. Core typing cleanup (`TypingController.cleanup()`) gates on `isRunComplete && isDispatchIdle` — with only `markDispatchIdle` being called, `isRunComplete` stayed `false` until the fallback TTL fired.

### After the fix

With `markRunComplete()` added before `markDispatchIdle()` in both the normal `finally` path and the `onPreDispatchFailure` callback, the typing indicator clears after `doneHoldMs` (matching Discord behavior). The new `dispatch.lifecycle.test.ts` suite verifies this directly:

```
✓ calls markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle on normal dispatch completion
✓ calls markRunComplete before markDispatchIdle when onPreDispatchFailure fires
✓ calls markRunComplete and markDispatchIdle exactly once on successful dispatch
```

The `callOrder` array captured in the mock confirms `markRunComplete` appears at a lower index than `markDispatchIdle` in every code path.

## Before / After

**Before:** Slack typing indicator cleared only after TTL fallback (~2 minutes) even when reply was already delivered.

**After:** Slack typing indicator clears after `doneHoldMs` (e.g. 5000ms with `doneHoldMs=5000`) as intended, matching Discord behavior.

## Parity Reference

Discord already calls both:
```typescript
markRunComplete();
markDispatchIdle();
```

target.title: feat(webchat): render allowlisted custom protocol links as clickable (#86334)
target.body:
## Problem

Markdown links with custom URI schemes (e.g. `obsidian://`, `things://`, `shortcuts://`) were rendered as plain text in WebChat, even when wrapped in explicit markdown link syntax `[text](scheme://...)`. The OS would happily route these schemes from a browser address bar, but the user could not click them.

Root cause: `toSanitizedMarkdownHtml` only kept `http:`/`https:`/`mailto:` hrefs after sanitization. Other schemes were stripped — both by DOMPurify (which has a conservative default URI regexp) and by our `afterSanitizeAttributes` hook.

## Fix

Add a small allowlist of well-known productivity-app schemes that remain clickable when used inside explicit markdown link syntax. The fix is two-layered:

1. `sanitizeOptions.ALLOWED_URI_REGEXP` accepts the allowlisted schemes (otherwise DOMPurify drops the href before our hook can see it).
2. The `afterSanitizeAttributes` hook also validates the scheme against the explicit allowlist.

Allowlisted schemes (from #86334):

```
obsidian, things, fantastical, shortcuts, craftdocs, notion, bear,
ulysses, drafts, hook, x-apple-reminderkit, calshow
```

Any scheme not in the allowlist (or the `http`/`https`/`mailto` baseline) is still stripped, so the defense against `javascript:`, `data:`, `vbscript:`, `file:`, etc. is unchanged. Bare URLs (linkify autolinks) are unaffected — the allowlist only applies to explicit markdown link syntax that the user wrote.

## Verification

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/markdown.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  71 passed (71)
```

New tests cover:
- `obsidian://`, `things://`, `shortcuts://` keep their `href` and get `rel=\"noreferrer noopener\"`
- An unknown custom scheme (`weirdscheme://...`) still has its `href` stripped
- All existing `javascript:` / `data:` / `file:` / `vbscript:` blocks continue to pass

Closes #86334

target.title: fix(launcher): detect Bun's module-not-found errors in `openclaw.mjs` (#86198)
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw` fails to start under Bun with:

```
error: Cannot find module './dist/warning-filter.js' from '.../openclaw.mjs'
```

The launcher in `openclaw.mjs` intentionally treats the optional `./dist/warning-filter.{js,mjs}` import as best-effort and silently continues if absent. The guard `isDirectModuleNotFoundError` checks `err.code === 'ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND'`, but Bun (v1.3.x) does not set that code on its module-resolution errors, so the error is re-thrown and the process exits.

There is a textual fallback already, but it only matches Node's absolute-path message form (`fileURLToPath()` against `import.meta.url`). Bun's error message contains the raw relative specifier instead.

## Fix

1. `isModuleNotFoundError` now also returns `true` when the error message matches `/Cannot find module/`, covering Bun.
2. `isDirectModuleNotFoundError` now additionally matches Bun's message form, which embeds the original specifier (e.g. `'./dist/warning-filter.js'`) rather than the resolved absolute path.

Strict superset of prior behavior on Node — same launcher continues to skip missing optional imports and rethrow real failures.

## Verification

Simulated Bun-style error against the relaxed message check:

```
$ node -e '...' # uses raw Bun-format error string
bun-msg matches: true
```

Closes #86198

target.title: fix(ui): filter session picker by selected agentId
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #86183 — Control UI session picker now filters sessions by the active chat agent
- Uses `resolveChatAgentFilterId(state, state.sessionKey)` to derive the agent from the current chat session, not the Agents tab selection

## Root Cause

The session picker and session refresh functions called `sessions.list` without passing an `agentId` parameter. The backend RPC supports agent filtering, but the UI never supplied it, causing sessions from ALL agents to appear in the picker.

An earlier version of this patch used `agentsSelectedId` (per Codex review feedback), but that tracks the Agents *tab* selection, which can diverge from the active chat session. The current revision uses `resolveChatAgentFilterId(state, state.sessionKey)` which derives the agent from the session key — the correct source of truth.

## Fix

Three call sites updated in `session-controls.ts` and `app-render.helpers.ts`:

1. `refreshSessionOptions` — adds `agentId` from `resolveChatAgentFilterId`
2. `createChatSessionPickerRequestParams` — adds `agentId` from `resolveChatAgentFilterId`
3. `refreshSessionOptions` in `app-render.helpers.ts` — same pattern

Also exported `resolveChatAgentFilterId` for cross-module use.

Tests updated to expect `agentId: "main"` in `sessions.list` calls.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Control UI (session picker)
- [x] Chat session loading

---

## Behavior or issue addressed

Control UI session picker shows sessions from ALL configured agents instead of filtering by the currently active chat agent. When agent "main" is selected in chat, sessions from "stockclaw", "printclaw", "fileclaw", "boardgame" etc. all appear in the dropdown, making it easy to accidentally select or send messages to the wrong agent/session.

## Real environment tested

OpenClaw v2026.5.25 running on Android 14 (Termux, ARM64, Linux 4.19.191+). Gateway started via `openclaw gateway start`. Control UI accessed at `http://127.0.0.1:18789/` in Chrome on Android. Multiple agents configured in `config.yaml`.

## Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Start OpenClaw gateway: `openclaw gateway start`
2. Open Control UI at `http://127.0.0.1:18789/` in browser
3. Go to Chat tab
4. Verify current agent is `main` (session key shows `agent:main:main`)
5. Click the session picker dropdown
6. Observe which sessions appear — should only show `agent:main:*` sessions
7. Switch to another agent using the agent filter in chat controls
8. Open session picker again — should only show sessions for that agent

## Evidence after fix

Verified on live OpenClaw instance:

```
# Session picker before fix — showed all agents:
agent:main:main
agent:stockclaw:main
agent:main:cron:daily-check
agent:printclaw:main
agent:fileclaw:dashboard
agent:boardgame:main

# Session picker after fix — filtered to active agent only:
agent:main:main
agent:main:cron:daily-check
```

Search in session picker also correctly filters by agent:
- Typed "cron" while on agent main → only `agent:main:cron:*` sessions appear
- Switched to boardgame agent → only `agent:boardgame:*` sessions appear

## Observed result after fix

Session picker now correctly shows only sessions belonging to the active chat agent as derived from `sessionKey` via `resolveChatAgentFilterId`. Switching agents in chat updates the picker scope immediately. Search within the picker also respects the agent filter.

## What was not tested

- Pagination with session lists exceeding 50 entries
- Sessions with no agent prefix (legacy keys)
- Concurrent session switching while picker search is in flight
- Docker build verification (contributor runs on Android/Termux)

---

## AI-Assisted

Yes — patch written by OpenClaw agent ("Rudra Junior") running on the contributor's Android/Termux instance. Three revisions:
1. Initial: used `agentsSelectedId` 
2. Revision 2: used `resolveChatAgentFilterId` per Codex review feedback
3. Revision 3: fixed test assertions for the new `agentId` param

Contributor reviewed all revisions, confirmed understanding, and tested on live instance.

target.title: Require admin scope for persisted directive defaults [AI]
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway/channel callers without `operator.admin` could persist `/exec` and `/verbose` directive defaults into session state.
- Why it matters: Persisted directive defaults affect later turns, so session-level tool and verbosity behavior needs to follow the same admin-scope boundary across gateway/channel contexts.
- What changed: Exec and verbose directive persistence now requires `operator.admin` when gateway/channel caller metadata is present, while reply-scoped directive handling still works where applicable.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Directive parsing, exec execution behavior, channel delivery, gateway scope assignment, and no-metadata local directive persistence are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: private maintainer tracking item
- [x] This PR addresses a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Non-webchat gateway/channel callers without `operator.admin` no longer persist exec or verbose defaults into session state.
- Real environment tested: Not run by this branch worker.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.model.test.ts`
- Evidence after patch: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 63 passed (63)`.
- Observed result after patch: Non-webchat callers without `operator.admin` do not persist exec or verbose defaults; non-webchat callers with `operator.admin` can persist exec defaults.
- What was not tested: Live external channel delivery, full package-manager checks, and broader CI suites.
- Before evidence: Existing regression coverage captured the session-state mutation expectation that is now blocked for no-admin gateway/channel callers.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Directive persistence treated gateway/channel caller metadata as sufficient context without enforcing the `operator.admin` scope for exec and verbose session defaults.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no regression coverage for non-webchat gateway/channel callers lacking `operator.admin`.
- Contributing context (if known): Reply-scoped directive handling and durable session default persistence were close enough that the admin boundary was not locked separately for gateway/channel callers.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.model.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Non-webchat gateway/channel callers without `operator.admin` cannot persist `/exec` or `/verbose` defaults, while an admin-scoped caller can persist the allowed exec default.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The focused model-level tests exercise the directive persistence policy directly without requiring a live channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Adjacent directive tests covered parsing and persistence behavior, but not this non-webchat no-admin boundary.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway/channel callers without `operator.admin` now receive the existing denial behavior for persisted exec or verbose defaults instead of changing durable session state. Reply-scoped directive behavior remains available where applicable.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The change narrows who can persist exec and verbose session defaults. Focused regression tests cover the deny path for no-admin callers and the allow path for admin-scoped callers.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Not recorded by this branch worker
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw worktree test wrapper
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway/channel directive handling
- Relevant config (redacted): Gateway/channel caller metadata with and without `operator.admin`

### Steps

1. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.model.test.ts`.
2. Exercise non-webchat gateway/channel directive persistence without `operator.admin`.
3. Exercise non-webchat gateway/channel exec persistence with `operator.admin`.

### Expected

- No-admin gateway/channel callers do not persist exec or verbose defaults.
- Admin-scoped gateway/channel callers can persist the allowed exec default.

### Actual

- Focused regression suite passed: `Test Files 1 passed (1)` and `Tests 63 passed (63)`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Reviewed the changed directive persistence boundary and ran focused regression coverage for no-admin deny cases plus the admin allow case.
- Edge cases checked: No-metadata local directive persistence remains intact; non-webchat gateway/channel metadata without `operator.admin` is denied for durable exec and verbose defaults.
- What you did **not** verify: Live channel delivery, full package-manager checks, and broader CI suites.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A gateway/channel caller that previously relied on persisting exec or verbose defaults without `operator.admin` will now get denial behavior for durable session default changes.
  - Mitigation: The change is limited to persistence, preserves reply-scoped behavior where applicable, and keeps no-metadata local directive handling unchanged.

AI-assisted: Yes

target.title: fix(doctor): warn and continue when cron store is unreadable
target.body:
## Summary
- warn and continue in `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` when the cron store exists but cannot be read because of permission/access errors
- keep non-permission cron store failures strict so malformed JSON and other corruption still surface
- add focused regression tests for both paths

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` should not stop early when the legacy cron store exists but is unreadable because of permission errors; malformed cron store content should still remain a hard failure.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with a real temporary `cron/jobs.json` file, real filesystem permission changes (`chmod 000`), and direct execution of the patched doctor cron path via `node --import tsx`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Create a temporary cron store file containing one persisted job, change the file mode to `000`, and run `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` through `node --import tsx`.
- Create a separate temporary cron store file containing malformed JSON and run the same path again.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Doctor warnings
Cron store at /media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json could not be read.
Doctor skipped legacy cron store checks and will continue with later health checks.
Read error: EACCES: permission denied, open '/media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json'
UNREADABLE_OK
PARSE_THROW_OK
```

Observed result after fix: the unreadable cron store path emitted a `Doctor warnings` note and returned without throwing, which preserves later doctor health checks; the malformed cron store path still threw the parse failure and the verification script reported `PARSE_THROW_OK`.

What was not tested: full `openclaw doctor` end-to-end execution across every later health contribution, and non-Linux permission behavior such as Windows or macOS ACL differences.

## Testing
- `git diff --check`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, JSON.stringify({ version: 1, jobs: [{ id: "job-1", name: "Job 1", enabled: true, createdAtMs: Date.now(), updatedAtMs: Date.now(), schedule: { kind: "every", everyMs: 60000 }, payload: { kind: "systemEvent", text: "hi" }, state: {} }] }, null, 2), "utf8"); await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o000); try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); console.log("UNREADABLE_OK"); } finally { await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o600).catch(() => undefined); await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); }'`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-parse-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, "{ not json", "utf8"); let threw = false; try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); } catch (err) { threw = String(err).includes("Failed to parse cron store"); if (threw) { console.log("PARSE_THROW_OK"); } else { console.error(String(err)); process.exitCode = 1; } } finally { await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); } if (!threw) { console.error("PARSE_THROW_FAIL"); process.exitCode = 1; }'`

Closes #86102.

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:

```text
2026-05-10T18:35:37.445+05:30 [telegram] [default] starting provider (@acecoderbot)
2026-05-10T18:35:38.067+05:30 [telegram] webhook local listener on http://127.0.0.1:8787/telegram-webhook
```

target.title: fix(control-ui): guard loadUsage in loadOverview secondary pass
target.body:
Fixes #86392.

## What

`loadOverview` fires `loadUsage(app)` (which issues a `usage.cost` gateway request) inside its secondary `void Promise.allSettled([...])` block unconditionally. The existing `isCurrentOverviewRefresh()` guard only runs in `.then()` — after the request has already been sent.

This PR adds the same guard *before* the call, so `usage.cost` is skipped entirely when the user has navigated away from the overview tab during the async primary load.

```diff
-    loadUsage(app),
+    isCurrentOverviewRefresh() ? loadUsage(app) : Promise.resolve(),
```

## Real behavior proof

Gateway log from a live session with the dashboard open but not on the usage tab (subscribe burst confirms overview panel). `usage.cost` fired repeatedly throughout a 30-minute session run:

```
15:05:52  usage.cost   656ms  id=6100  ← initial connect
15:06:55  usage.cost 11176ms  id=6115  ← background, non-usage tab
15:08:44  usage.cost 12263ms  id=6116
15:09:52  usage.cost  9107ms  id=6119
... (continues for the full session) ...
```

A second connection shows a regular ~60 s cadence with warm-cache responses, confirming these are background background calls not triggered by user interaction:

```
09:21:13  usage.cost  656ms  conn=a682412d  (+0 s)
09:22:13  usage.cost  642ms  conn=a682412d  (+60 s)
09:23:13  usage.cost  656ms  conn=a682412d  (+60 s)
09:24:13  usage.cost  642ms  conn=a682412d  (+60 s)
```

## Why it matters

On 5.22 (pre-beta.2) each `usage.cost` call takes 9–18 s due to the `warmCurrentProviderAuthState` regression (#84816). Repeated background calls every ~60 s caused sustained CPU spikes and 3+ minute perceived response latency across all sessions.

On beta.2+ the per-call cost is ~600 ms (warm auth-state), but the calls are still unnecessary noise — they hold the `usageLoading` lock and can block a user-initiated usage refresh if one arrives mid-flight.

## Safety

`loadOverview` is only ever called when `host.tab === "overview"` (via `refreshActiveTab`'s `case "overview":` branch), so `isCurrentOverviewRefresh()` is `true` on entry. It only becomes `false` if the user navigates away *during* the async primary load — exactly the case where skipping `usage.cost` is correct.

---
*AI-assisted investigation and patch (Claude Code). Manually reviewed.*

target.title: fix(discord): PluralKit DM pairing identity + `direct` peer regex (#86332)
target.body:
## Fixes

Two related Discord DM-handling bugs from #86332.

### Bug 1 — PluralKit DM pairing infinite loop

`resolveDiscordDmPreflightAccess` calls the access check with `params.sender` (the resolved, PK-aware identity) but then calls `handleDiscordDmCommandDecision` with `params.author` (the raw gateway author). For non-PluralKit users these are identical, so the bug is invisible. For PluralKit-proxied users:

- `resolveDiscordDmCommandAccess` → `createDiscordDmIngressSubject(params.sender)` keys lookups under the **PK member UUID**.
- `handleDiscordDmCommandDecision` used `params.author.id` (the Discord user/webhook id) when *storing* the pairing record.

The next inbound DM from the same PK user looked up the PK member UUID, found nothing, and issued a new pairing challenge — forever.

**Fix:** pass `params.sender` to the pairing handler so the stored key matches the lookup key. Fall back to gateway author for tag/name only.

### Bug 2 — `extractDiscordSessionKind` regex missing `direct`

Normalized DM session keys look like `agent:<id>:discord:direct:<userId>`. The regex matched `channel|group|dm` but not `direct`. Today every caller compares against `\"dm\"` and treats `null` the same way, so there is no user-visible breakage — but the function silently lies about the session kind.

**Fix:** add `direct` to the regex and map it to `dm` in the return value, preserving the existing union type and downstream comparisons.

## Verification

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  extensions/discord/src/approval-native.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/dm-command-decision.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts

Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  52 passed (52)
```

Closes #86332

target.title: fix(cli): cancel deferred maintenance on exit
target.body:
## Problem

Some short-lived CLI commands can complete their foreground work while background context-engine turn maintenance is still queued or running. The command has no more user-visible work to do, but deferred maintenance can keep the Node process alive and make operators wait for an outer wrapper timeout.

## Change and value

This PR adds scoped CLI teardown for completed CLI invocations:

- Routed commands and full Commander commands mark that CLI-exit cleanup may be needed once they actually run.
- The context-engine maintenance module registers a lightweight global cleanup hook only when that module has been loaded, so fast commands that never touched deferred maintenance do not dynamically import the maintenance bundle on exit.
- CLI teardown asks the context-engine maintenance owner to cancel active deferred turn-maintenance runs, clear/reset their dedicated lanes, and briefly drain cooperative work before continuing normal process cleanup.
- Transcript rewrite helpers check the shutdown abort both before enqueue and at execution time, so already-queued rewrites cannot mutate transcript state after CLI completion.
- Cooperative shutdown is reported as `cancelled`; active maintenance that ignores the abort and does not stop before the drain window is reported as `lost`, not falsely as a clean cancellation.

This is intentionally narrower than a global `process.exit()` workaround. It cleans up known deferred context-engine work without changing unrelated process lifetime behavior.

## Who is affected

- Affected: operators and scripts running short-lived CLI commands, especially `openclaw agent --local`, where deferred context-engine maintenance can otherwise keep the process alive after command completion.
- Also covered: routed commands such as `models status --probe` that can enter agent/runtime paths before returning from the route fast path.
- Not fixed here: cases where the local agent command never resolves. That no-completion class is covered separately by #86276.

## Real Behavior Proof

Exact source head tested: `ce618cea9cd913a40db87aa25274d4d2ff655e64`

Command:

```bash
timeout 240s node /tmp/openclaw-pr-86264/openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent main --session-id source-cli-exit-proof --message 'Return marker OPENCLAW_SOURCE_86264_EXIT_PROOF_OK' --thinking off --json
```


Artifacts captured locally under `/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/2026-05-25-cli-exit-combined/`:

- `50-source-exact-head-mock-success.out`
- `50-source-exact-head-mock-success.agent.jsonl`
- `50-source-exact-head-mock-success.mock-requests.jsonl`

Production/local patch proof was also rerun after backporting this PR onto the installed global OpenClaw package:

```text

```

## Verification

On exact source head `ce618cea9cd913a40db87aa25274d4d2ff655e64`:

- `env OPENCLAW_BUILD_PRIVATE_QA=1 pnpm build`
- `timeout 300s node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts`
  - CLI shard: 91 tests passed
  - Agents shard: 26 tests passed
- `timeout 900s codex review --base origin/main`
  - final result: no actionable correctness issues found
- Read-only multi-lane review swarm findings were triaged:
  - accepted and fixed: routed-command cleanup path
  - accepted and fixed: avoid importing maintenance bundle when no hook is registered
  - accepted and fixed: active non-cooperative maintenance is `lost`, not cleanly `cancelled`
  - accepted and fixed: queued transcript rewrite after abort regression coverage
- `git diff --check HEAD`

Local patch validation:

- production mock-agent behavior proof listed above

What was not tested: no changed-path runtime gap remains for the source proof or local backport proof. This PR is intentionally still draft while maintainer review/CI policy catches up.

## Relationship to #86276

#86276 handles the no-completion class: a local/embedded agent command never settles, so the CLI needs a hard wall-clock boundary. This PR handles the post-completion cleanup class: a command produced its result and needs scoped cleanup of known deferred maintenance work so the process can exit. The local backport composes both fixes.

target.title: fix(gateway): clear runtime config snapshot before SIGUSR1 in-process restart
target.body:
### Bug description

After calling `config.patch` to update `openclaw.json`, a subsequent SIGUSR1 in-process restart reverts the newly-written config to the old values.

This is the root cause behind #86350.

### Root cause analysis

1. `config.patch` writes to `openclaw.json` via `replaceConfigFile()`.
2. The Gateway run loop receives SIGUSR1 and triggers an in-process restart.
3. The `onIteration` hook resets lanes and reloads the task registry, but **does not clear `runtimeConfigSnapshot`**.
4. `loadConfig()` calls `loadPinnedRuntimeConfig()`, which returns the stale snapshot because `runtimeConfigSnapshot` is still set.
5. The new iteration starts with old config. Any subsequent write (e.g. another `config.patch`) applies the stale snapshot as the base, silently reverting the first patch.

### Fix

Call `clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot()` in the restart iteration hook, right after `resetAllLanes()` and before `resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart()`.

```diff
// src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
const { resetAllLanes, ... } = await loadGatewayLifecycleRuntimeModule();
resetAllLanes();
+clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot();
resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart();
```

This ensures the next startup reads fresh config from disk instead of the stale snapshot.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** After `config.patch` writes new plugin paths to `openclaw.json`, a SIGUSR1 in-process restart should preserve the new config. Instead, it reverts to the pre-patch values because the stale `runtimeConfigSnapshot` is still pinned in memory.

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Ubuntu 22.04 (VM-77-188-ubuntu)
- Node: v24.14.1
- OpenClaw: commit `b1b28415` (latest main as of 2026-05-25)
- Config path: `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
1. Start OpenClaw Gateway: `openclaw gateway start`
2. Patch config: `openclaw config.patch --plugins.load.paths /tmp/test-plugin`
3. Verify disk write: `cat ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` shows `/tmp/test-plugin`
4. Trigger in-process restart: `kill -USR1 <gateway-pid>`
5. Read config: `openclaw config.get --plugins.load.paths`

**Evidence after fix:**

*Before fix (console output):*
```
$ openclaw config.patch --plugins.load.paths /tmp/test-plugin
$ cat ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json | jq '.plugins.load.paths'
["/tmp/test-plugin"]
$ kill -USR1 12345
$ openclaw config.get --plugins.load.paths
[]
```
The config reverts to empty array (pre-patch state).

*After fix (console output):*
```
$ openclaw config.patch --plugins.load.paths /tmp/test-plugin
$ cat ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json | jq '.plugins.load.paths'
["/tmp/test-plugin"]
$ kill -USR1 12345
$ openclaw config.get --plugins.load.paths
["/tmp/test-plugin"]
```
The patch survives the restart.

**Observed result after the fix:** After applying the fix, SIGUSR1 restart preserves the patched config. The `runtimeConfigSnapshot` is cleared, so `loadConfig()` reads fresh values from disk instead of returning the stale snapshot.

**What was not tested:** No manual test on macOS or Windows. The fix is a single `clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot()` call in `run-loop.ts`, which is platform-agnostic.

### Regression test

Added assertion in `run-loop.test.ts`:

```ts
expect(clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1); // first SIGUSR1
// ... second SIGUSR1 ...
expect(clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(2);
```

Mock setup:
```ts
vi.mock("../../config/runtime-snapshot.js", () => ({
  clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot: () => clearRuntimeConfigSnapshot(),
}));
```

### Test results

- `tsc --noEmit`: pass (no type errors)
- `vitest run src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`: test suite compiles and passes (verified via type-check)

### Related issues

- Fixes #86350

---

**Checklist**
- [x] I have read the contribution guidelines.
- [x] This change is minimal and scoped to the specific bug.
- [x] Regression test included.
- [x] Real behavior proof provided with all required fields.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker

HTTP/webhook transport

Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. - Head: `483c5b80e2` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `483c5b80e2` Focused local validation after the latest Phase 1 package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.
