target.title: fix: stop repeated heartbeat response loops
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Prevents a heartbeat turn from continuing to issue repeated `heartbeat_respond` calls after a valid response has already been recorded.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #86324 reports a main-session heartbeat turn that repeated `heartbeat_respond` 810 times and burned very large token/cache-read volume without useful user-visible work.

What is the intended outcome?

- A valid `heartbeat_respond` records the heartbeat outcome once and terminates the heartbeat-triggered model turn.
- A second call to the same heartbeat response tool instance is rejected as already recorded.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Changing default heartbeat session routing or moving heartbeat work out of the main session.
- Adding new heartbeat configuration knobs.

What does success look like?

- Silent `notify=false` and visible `notify=true` heartbeat responses still resolve normally, while repeated response-tool loops stop after the first valid response.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether aborting the active session after a heartbeat response is recorded preserves the structured heartbeat payload and avoids reporting the turn as interrupted.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #86324

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #86324

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Autonomous OpenClaw P1 repair work approved by Uday for `openclaw/openclaw` P1 issues.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: repeated `heartbeat_respond` loop inside a heartbeat turn.
- Real environment tested: local macOS checkout from `openclaw/openclaw`, branch `fix/86324-heartbeat-respond-loop`, Node v25.9.0, pnpm v11.2.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx -e 'import { createHeartbeatResponseTool } from "./src/agents/tools/heartbeat-response-tool.ts"; const tool = createHeartbeatResponseTool(); const first = await tool.execute("heartbeat-1", { outcome: "no_change", notify: false, summary: "Heartbeat check complete: git clean, daily memory exists, no async tasks pending." }); console.log("first heartbeat_respond status=" + first.details.status + " outcome=" + first.details.outcome + " notify=" + first.details.notify); try { await tool.execute("heartbeat-2", { outcome: "no_change", notify: false, summary: "Heartbeat check complete: git clean, daily memory exists, no async tasks pending." }); console.log("second heartbeat_respond unexpectedly recorded"); process.exitCode = 1; } catch (err) { console.log("second heartbeat_respond rejected=" + String(err instanceof Error ? err.message : err)); }'
```

- Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the local OpenClaw code-path probe:

```text
first heartbeat_respond status=recorded outcome=no_change notify=false
second heartbeat_respond rejected=heartbeat_respond already recorded for this turn
```

- Observed result after fix: the first heartbeat response records the structured no-change result, and the immediate repeated response is rejected with the per-turn guard instead of recording another response.
- What was not tested: a live provider/model reproduction of the original 810-call incident was not available.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this console probe exercises the OpenClaw heartbeat response tool path directly; it does not reproduce the unavailable production transcript loop with the original provider/model. Default Clawpatch provider review also failed locally with OpenAI Codex `401 Unauthorized`, so mock-provider Clawpatch review/report was used as a deterministic fallback and found 0 findings.
- Before evidence: before the implementation, the same second `heartbeat_respond` call recorded another response instead of being rejected.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/heartbeat-response-tool.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.subscribeembeddedpisession.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/heartbeat-response-tool.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.subscribeembeddedpisession.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.tool-response.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status.test.ts src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `git diff --check`
- `/Users/miya/.openclaw/bin/clawpatch doctor --root /Users/miya/Desktop/openclaw-issue-86324`
- `/Users/miya/.openclaw/bin/clawpatch map --root /Users/miya/Desktop/openclaw-issue-86324`
- `/Users/miya/.openclaw/bin/clawpatch review --root /Users/miya/Desktop/openclaw-issue-86324 --limit 3 --jobs 1`
- `/Users/miya/.openclaw/bin/clawpatch review --root /Users/miya/Desktop/openclaw-issue-86324 --limit 3 --jobs 1 --provider mock`
- `/Users/miya/.openclaw/bin/clawpatch report --root /Users/miya/Desktop/openclaw-issue-86324`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage that a heartbeat response tool instance rejects a second `heartbeat_respond` call in the same turn.
- Added coverage that the embedded subscriber records the heartbeat payload and invokes the terminal response callback only once.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The repeated-response test failed because the second call resolved as another recorded response.
- The subscriber callback test failed because no terminal callback was invoked.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, for heartbeat turns that call `heartbeat_respond`: the response is now terminal for that heartbeat turn.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, tool execution behavior changes narrowly for the heartbeat response tool.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Aborting the active session after the heartbeat response could accidentally classify the heartbeat turn as interrupted or lose the structured heartbeat payload.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The runner only enables terminal abort behavior for `trigger === "heartbeat"`.
- The prompt catch path clears the abort state for this terminal heartbeat-response abort.
- Existing heartbeat runner/tool-response tests and trajectory-status tests still pass.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- CI and maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI results.
- Optional maintainer follow-up for the default Clawpatch provider auth failure in this local environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- No PR bot/reviewer comments yet.

AI-assisted: yes, implemented by Codex/Vector.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d1de4ad7c8` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 4 - Constrained/device-bound provider capabilities - Role: Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. - Head: `0295ff8709` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `0295ff8709` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds Phase 4 constrained/device-bound provider metadata to the Channel Broker contract. - Covers WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles-backed iMessage bridges as broker provider capabilities, not native plugin rewrites. - Stacks on Phase 3 official/app capability matrix (#86164), Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86114 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86148, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds `constraints` and `badges` to broker platform/provider capabilities. - Models constrained provider facts such as: - `businessApi`, `cloudApi`, `providerHosted` - `deviceBound`, `linkedDevice`, `qrPairing`, `sessionFragile` - `selfHosted`, `phoneNumberRequired`, `signalCli` - `macHostRequired`, `messagesSignedIn`, `privateApiOptional`, `privateApiRequired` - `externalBridge` - Exposes resolved provider capabilities in channel-broker status snapshots so UI/status can show constrained-provider badges. - Adds WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage target proof through `channel-broker`. - Documents constrained provider expectations in Channel Broker, SDK, WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles migration docs. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Constrained providers expose hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed limits through broker `constraints`/`badges`, and WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage still route through provider-owned broker requests. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that evaluated constrained-provider capability support and called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` for `broker:whatsapp:direct:15551234567`, `broker:signal:direct:+15551234567`, and `broker:imessage:group:chat-guid-1?threadId=imessage-thread-1`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "constrainedProviders": { "whatsappBusiness": true, "whatsappDeviceBound": false, "signalDeviceBound": true }, "outbound": [ { "label": "whatsapp-direct", "platform": "whatsapp", "conversation": { "id": "15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "signal-direct", "platform": "signal", "conversation": { "id": "+15551234567", "type": "direct" } }, { "label": "imessage-group", "platform": "imessage", "conversation": { "id": "chat-guid-1", "type": "group", "threadId": "imessage-thread-1" } } ] } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path distinguished WhatsApp Business-style support from device-bound WhatsApp requirements, accepted Signal's self-hosted/device-bound constraint set, and produced broker provider requests for WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. - **What was not tested:** Live WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, or BlueBubbles-backed provider traffic; this PR proves the broker contract/schema/docs layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/status.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md docs/channels/signal.md docs/channels/imessage.md docs/channels/imessage-from-bluebubbles.md` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not implement Phase 5 legacy-plugin freeze. - Next phase: #86115 freezes new legacy native-channel feature work after broker migration paths exist. This PR supplies the constrained-provider metadata needed before that freeze can be credible.

target.title: fix(cron-cli): accept '+duration' form in `--at` as documented (#86230)
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw cron add --help` documents `--at` as:

```
--at <when>  Run once at time (ISO with offset, or +duration). Use --tz for offset-less datetimes
```

But the validator rejected the documented `+duration` form:

```
$ openclaw cron add --at '+30m' ...
Error: Invalid --at. Use an ISO timestamp or a duration like 20m.
```

Scripts that lifted `+30m` from the help text silently failed to schedule (see #86230 for the downstream skill that hit this).

## Fix

- `parseAt` now accepts both `30m` and `+30m` (strips a single leading `+` before delegating to `parseDurationMs`). Backward compatible.
- Validator error message updated to mention both forms.
- Added unit tests for bare, leading-plus, and rejection cases.

## Test

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/cli/cron-cli/shared.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  24 passed (24)
```

Closes #86230

target.title: docs(telegram): note native progress draft control tradeoff
target.body:
## Summary

- Document that Telegram native tool-progress drafts use `sendMessageDraft` and can leave some clients, including Telegram Android, in a passive-viewing state while active.
- Recommend keeping `nativeToolProgress` disabled for workflows that need `/steer`, `/stop`, corrections, or mid-run follow-ups from the same chat.
- Point interactive users at the existing edited preview lane with `toolProgress: true` and `nativeToolProgress: false`.

Fixes #86195

## Tests

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm docs:check-links`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram docs now explain the native `sendMessageDraft` tool-progress tradeoff and the safer interactive-control configuration for users who need to steer or stop active runs from the same chat.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw docs checkout on macOS using the repository docs validation scripts against this patch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm docs:list`; `git diff --check`; `pnpm docs:check-mdx`; `pnpm docs:check-links`.

target.title: feat(exec): add normalized auto mode
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had separate native exec approval knobs and Codex-specific Guardian config, so `auto` meant different things depending on the harness.
- Why it matters: users need one normalized exec policy surface that maps cleanly to native OpenClaw exec and Codex app-server execution.
- What changed: added `tools.exec.mode`, native OpenClaw auto-review plumbing for exec approval misses, and Codex app-server mapping from OpenClaw `auto` to Guardian-reviewed approvals.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): existing `tools.exec.security` / `tools.exec.ask` remain supported, and explicit Codex app-server policy fields still override the normalized preset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): Native exec approval policy and Codex app-server approval policy evolved as separate configuration surfaces.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts`, `src/agents/exec-defaults.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts`, `src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: normalized exec mode mapping, native auto-review behavior, Codex Guardian mapping, and session/directive propagation.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests cover the policy resolver, native execution seam, Codex adapter seam, and persisted session API without a full live harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing exec allowlist/session tests were extended.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds preferred config `tools.exec.mode` with values `deny`, `allowlist`, `ask`, `auto`, and `full`.
- Adds `/exec mode=...` directive support.
- `tools.exec.mode="auto"` enables native OpenClaw auto review for exec approval misses.
- Codex app-server sessions map OpenClaw `auto` to Guardian-reviewed approvals when no explicit Codex app-server mode/policy overrides are set.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
OpenClaw exec policy -> security/ask only
Codex app-server -> separate guardian/yolo preset

After:
tools.exec.mode=auto -> native OpenClaw auto reviewer
                     -> Codex app-server Guardian-reviewed approvals
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `tools.exec.mode="auto"` can approve low-risk read-only exec approval misses without a human prompt. The built-in reviewer fails closed to human review, avoids compound shell/mutation/network/privilege-sensitive commands, preserves legacy raw policy overrides, and Codex maps to its existing Guardian-reviewed approval posture.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Codex app-server harness
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.exec.mode="auto"`

### Steps

1. Configure `tools.exec.mode="auto"`.
2. Resolve exec defaults or issue `/exec mode=auto`.
3. Start a Codex app-server attempt without explicit app-server mode overrides.

### Expected

- Native OpenClaw exec uses auto-review semantics on approval misses.
- Codex app-server maps the normalized OpenClaw mode to Guardian-reviewed approvals.

### Actual

- Verified through targeted unit/seam tests.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted verification passed:

```text

```

Also passed:

```text

```

`pnpm check:changed` passed conflict markers, core/extension typechecks, core/extension lint, import cycles, and auth/body guards, then the changed-test wrapper terminated `test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts` after 180s with no output (`failed:143`). The touched targeted gateway test passed independently.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: exec mode normalization, config/session/directive propagation, Codex Guardian mapping, explicit override precedence, native auto-review allow/ask paths.
- Edge cases checked: legacy lower-scope `security` / `ask` overrides beat higher-scope `mode`; explicit Codex app-server fields override normalized OpenClaw `auto`.
- What you did **not** verify: live Codex app-server Guardian end-to-end approval flow and the full `pnpm check:changed` changed-test wrapper due the gateway shard no-output stall noted above.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `auto` could be interpreted as host routing because `tools.exec.host="auto"` already exists.

- Risk: native auto-review is too permissive.

- Risk: Codex-specific Guardian semantics leak into OpenClaw config.
  - Mitigation: Codex mapping happens inside the Codex extension adapter; OpenClaw-facing config remains `tools.exec.mode="auto"`.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API and regional plugin platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, including WeChat/Weixin, and it enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker

HTTP/webhook transport

Current head: `7bff662dee` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 3 official/app channel capability matrix for Channel Broker. - Keeps the work at the broker contract/catalog layer: no native channel migration defaults change in this PR. - Stacks on Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86113 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86144, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds canonical broker platform aliases while keeping `platform: string` open for provider-owned long-tail targets: - `teams` / `msteams` -> `microsoft-teams` - `googlechat` -> `google-chat` - `qq` -> `qqbot` - Aligns `channels.channel-broker.*.capabilities` config with the SDK capability shape: - `delivery` - `live` - `receive` - `native` - Adds focused proof for Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, LINE, Feishu, QQ bot, Zalo, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Twitch, IRC, Nostr, Tlon, and Synology Chat. - Documents the Phase 3 platform matrix and SDK capability shape. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Official/app channel aliases normalize through the broker catalog, and Phase 3 platforms route through provider-owned broker requests without enabling native migration defaults. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:teams:19:meeting-channel"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "teams-alias", "messageId": "native-teams-alias", "platform": "microsoft-teams", "conversation": { "id": "19:meeting-channel", "type": "channel" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path canonicalized the Teams alias to `microsoft-teams` and produced a provider-owned broker request with no platform-specific native extension branch. - **What was not tested:** Live Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, regional, or self-hosted provider traffic; this PR proves the broker catalog/schema/conformance layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` Note: `pnpm exec markdownlint-cli2 ...` was unavailable in the local workspace, so the documented `pnpm dlx` path was used. target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary

- Rebased PR #85941 onto current `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `b6b275575fc3bc91e64a87ace1f69f3b62e2e780`.
- Improves visible reply latency by removing hot-path dispatcher-idle waits from direct block reply delivery. Pending block delivery is now flushed only before later tool/progress callbacks where user-visible ordering matters.
- Schedules source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response, while serializing mirrors per session to preserve transcript chronology.
- Sends Telegram direct-message typing only after inbound body resolution and configured binding readiness, while still before expensive context/session construction, so dropped/no-reply turns do not show misleading typing.
- Prevents progress-mode text-only tool outputs from duplicating/restarting the live progress draft.
- Keeps slash-command and session-write maintenance out of the reply hot path where possible, including native handling for bare/unknown `/skill` commands and skip-full-maintenance session writes.
- Keeps timing diagnostics opt-in instead of always-on, covering reply timing and Codex app-server startup/tool timing instrumentation.
- Rebased Codex app-server profiler flag enablement onto the shared diagnostics flag matcher documented in `docs/diagnostics/flags.md`, so config flags, env flags, wildcards, and `OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0` disable override behave consistently.
- Caches repeated Codex workspace filesystem setup, removing the repeated `workspace=3.565s` cost observed inside the `3.977s` pre-dynamic startup sample.
- Avoids expensive provider/plugin runtime model normalization on hot paths unless explicitly needed, and keeps model visibility policy construction off plugin runtime hooks by default.
- Uses lightweight/static thinking catalog checks before hydrating the full runtime catalog, removing about `17.9s` of measured per-turn model/thinking-catalog startup tax from the captured hot path (`3.588s` default model + `14.387s` thinking catalog).
- Avoids live channel health probes in reply-adjacent paths by using `probe: false` for automatic gateway health refresh and WS post-connect refresh, removing the measured `11.2s` Discord `/users/@me` timeout from Telegram reply-adjacent work.
- Defers scheduled heartbeats when the same agent already has an active reply run, reducing reply/cron contention.
- Defers context-engine-owned budget compaction to background maintenance when supported, and treats no-op/under-target/deferred-background compaction as a skip instead of blocking preflight, shaving roughly `121s` from the compaction portion and about `140s` from inbound-to-assemble in the worst captured sample.
- Routes fallback compaction for OpenAI/Codex OAuth-backed sessions through the resolved Codex OAuth runtime provider, fixing the repeated missing `OPENAI_API_KEY` failure for Codex-auth runs.
- Documents non-obvious performance/ordering choices inline so future changes do not accidentally reintroduce the blocking behavior.


## Profiler flag control

Profiler timing is controlled by the standard diagnostics flag mechanism documented in `docs/diagnostics/flags.md`.

Enable every profiler-gated timing span for one gateway run:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable only reply-dispatch profiler spans:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=reply.profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable only Codex app-server startup/tool/thread profiler spans:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=codex.profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable profiler flags from config:

```json
{
  "diagnostics": {
    "flags": ["reply.profiler", "codex.profiler"]
  }
}
```

Disable profiler flags by removing them from `diagnostics.flags` and restarting the gateway. For a one-process hard disable, use:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0 openclaw gateway run
```

`OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0` overrides profiler flags from config as well as env for that process.

## Regression source

#83722 is the merged regression source for one major latency path. It correctly fixed same-channel pre-tool block ordering by waiting for block reply delivery before tool execution could continue, but the merged implementation put that delivery barrier directly on the hot block-reply callback path.

That meant lightweight/direct replies could wait for dispatcher idle or delivery bookkeeping even when no later tool/progress event was about to be emitted. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, slow channel I/O, transcript mirroring pressure, request-time compaction, model/runtime catalog hydration, or health-probe overhead, those hot-path waits were amplified into user-visible latency.

This PR preserves the ordering guarantee, but moves the wait to the point where ordering matters: before later tool/progress emission. Direct block replies can resolve without waiting on dispatcher idle, while subsequent events still flush pending block delivery first.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: direct visible replies should not be delayed by dispatcher idle waits, transcript mirroring, request-time context-engine compaction, always-on diagnostics, model/runtime catalog hydration, live health probes, hook relay startup, or cron contention while preserving block-before-tool/progress ordering.

Real environment tested: live OpenClaw gateway on Keshav's host, Telegram direct message and Telegram topic `547`. The PR currently has base `b6b275575fc3bc91e64a87ace1f69f3b62e2e780`, pushed head `08089bed40f08539d50c1911810165d5a89a2c8a`, and GitHub reports merge state `CLEAN`. Keshav built and restarted the current pushed head; the live gateway process below started after the build-info timestamp and is running the same `/home/clawuser/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789` entrypoint.

CI follow-up after the latest rebase: restored built-in static normalization for OpenRouter bare model ids and XAI legacy aliases even when manifest normalization is disabled, and guarded media-only reply state when `allowedModelCatalog` is absent. The formerly red `checks-node-agentic-commands-agent-channel`, `checks-node-auto-reply-reply-state-routing`, and `checks-node-auto-reply-reply-dispatch` shards are green on the current head.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: read live gateway journal and `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-25.log` for the current Telegram request, then checked the final rebased source head with `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on the eight review-touched files, `pnpm tsgo:core`, and `pnpm tsgo:extensions`. Focused regression Vitest batches also passed during review for compaction, reply dispatch, gateway send, Telegram dispatch/typing, heartbeat deferral, Codex app-server startup/tool behavior, and plugin runtime model selection.

Evidence after fix: copied live runtime output from Keshav's current Telegram request, redacted only by omitting unrelated log lines:

```

Observed result after fix: this live request reached resolver milestone in `4.168s`, embedded-run handoff in about `12.2s` from inbound, and Codex app-server thread binding in about `20.0-21.8s` from inbound even while another same-agent main heartbeat was active. The earlier measured start-of-PR problem was about `30.9s` before agent work became visible and `49.9s` inbound-to-prompt on the health-probe/compaction-heavy path; this live proof shows roughly `18.7s` shaved from the pre-agent/embedded handoff path and about `28s` shaved from the worst measured inbound-to-prompt-class path.

Measured blocker/delta details from the PR investigation:

- Request-time context-engine compaction: the early broken path received the Telegram message at `00:12:04.739`, did not finish compaction/assemble until `00:14:24.916`, and hit a `121087ms` compaction deadline after entering compaction. This PR defers context-engine-owned budget compaction to background maintenance where supported and treats no-op/under-target/background-deferred results as skips, shaving roughly `121s` from the compaction portion of that path and about `140s` from inbound-to-assemble in the worst captured sample.
- Discord `/users/@me` health probe: the `20:06` live run spent `11.2s` in a Discord identity fetch timeout after preflight. Automatic gateway health refresh and WS post-connect health refresh now use `probe:false`, so that external `@me` timeout is no longer paid by Telegram reply-adjacent work.
- Workspace filesystem setup: the `22:21` live run showed Codex pre-dynamic startup at `3.977s`, dominated by `workspace=3.565s`. The PR adds process-local Codex workspace mkdir caching so repeated app-server attempts stop re-paying roughly `3.6s` of filesystem setup cost.
- Model and thinking-catalog hydration: the `23:06` live run showed `reply.resolve_default_model=3.588s` and `reply.resolve_thinking_catalog=14.387s`. The PR disables plugin/provider normalization for hot-path default-model policy construction and uses lightweight/static thinking-catalog checks before hydrating the full runtime catalog, removing roughly `17.9s` of measured model/catalog startup tax from that path.
- Slash/session maintenance: command-only session preference writes previously paid full session maintenance over the sessions tree, measured at `1723ms`; the skip-maintenance path measured `78ms`, shaving about `1.6s` from slash-command persistence.
- Heartbeat/cron contention: prior live runs showed reply overlap with same-agent heartbeat/cron work and event-loop p99/max around `10-12s`. Scheduled heartbeats now defer while the same agent has an active reply run. The latest live proof is better but not solved: it still shows `eventLoopDelayP99Ms=3858.8`, max `10007.6`, when a main heartbeat overlaps the Telegram reply.
- Dynamic tool build: repeated live runs put runtime plus registered tool construction around `3s`; the current live proof still shows about `3.4s`. This PR removes avoidable surrounding blockers and keeps the diagnostic timing opt-in, but does not claim dynamic tool construction itself is fully eliminated.

What was not tested: live WhatsApp/Discord account roundtrip, or a completed final send for Telegram topic request `2209` at the moment this proof was captured. A completed current-head Telegram send after restart is recorded above for direct message `11298` -> `11299`.

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/profiler-flag.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact-reasons.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.post-connect-health.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-timing-tracker.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
