target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `7614f3bcfb` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `7614f3bcfb` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR.

Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2C Discord broker routing proof on top of #86154, #86153, and #86096. - Normalizes broker-routed Discord DMs and channel threads without importing native Discord extension internals. - Preserves non-channel conversation type in canonical broker routes so Discord DMs remain direct routes. ## Rollout Context Closes #86111 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86154 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86139, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Discord defaults stay unchanged. Existing prefix ownership still keeps `discord:` owned by the native Discord plugin when installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:discord:...` and the broker alias path when native Discord is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and provider-owned ids. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:discord:user:123456789012345678"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "discord-dm", "messageId": "native-discord-dm", "platform": "discord", "conversation": { "id": "123456789012345678", "type": "direct" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced a direct Discord broker provider request rather than an ambiguous channel-looking target. - **What was not tested:** Live Discord API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can represent Discord DMs and channel threads through `channel-broker` using stable broker semantics: provider platform `discord`, direct/channel conversation type, conversation id, and optional thread id. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-discord-routing` - Head: `56bc4818a1` (current Discord proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:user:123456789012345678`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "discord"`, `conversation.type: "direct"`, and `conversation.id: "123456789012345678"`. 3. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:channel:222222222222222222?threadId=333333333333333333`. 4. Verified the provider request used channel conversation id plus `threadId`. 5. Resolved the outbound session route for the Discord DM target. 6. Verified the route stayed direct and canonicalized to `discord:direct%3A123456789012345678`. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 13 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Discord DMs and threads now flow through broker target parsing and provider request semantics, not through core or native Discord extension branches. ### Not Tested - Live Discord API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d1de4ad7c8` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `d1de4ad7c8` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack

GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. target.title: fix(memory): export LanceDB artifacts for wiki bridge
target.body:
## Summary
- add shared workspace public artifact listing for memory bridge consumers
- make memory-lancedb export a deterministic Markdown bridge artifact for local file-backed dbPath values
- derive LanceDB bridge artifact agent ids from configured memory workspaces
- skip LanceDB table Markdown export for URI-backed dbPath values while preserving workspace memory artifacts
- preserve workspace memory artifacts when memory-lancedb is the active memory plugin
- preserve only public artifact providers discovered during activate:false loads without retaining prompt, flush, or runtime state
- render native wiki links without `.md` targets

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed**: `memory-wiki` bridge mode should continue exporting/importing public memory artifacts when `memory-lancedb` is the active memory plugin, including the LanceDB memory export and workspace memory Markdown artifacts.
- **Real setup tested**: Live OpenClaw install on my local workstation, with the equivalent local LanceDB/wiki bridge repair applied. This PR upstreams that repaired behavior into source.
- **Exact steps or command run after fix**: Ran `openclaw wiki bridge import`, `openclaw wiki status`, and `openclaw wiki lint` against the live OpenClaw setup after the repair.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied terminal output from the live setup:

```text
$ openclaw wiki bridge import
Bridge import synced 35 artifacts across 2 workspaces (0 new, 1 updated, 34 unchanged, 0 removed). Indexes refreshed (2 files).

$ openclaw wiki status
Wiki vault mode: bridge Vault: ready (~/.openclaw/wiki/main) Render mode: native Obsidian CLI: missing Bridge: enabled (35 exported artifacts) Unsafe local: disabled Pages: 101 sources, 1 entities, 9 concepts, 5 syntheses, 10 reports Source provenance: 0 native, 35 bridge, 0 bridge-events, 0 unsafe-local, 66 other

$ openclaw wiki lint
Linted wiki vault at ~/.openclaw/wiki/main (0 issues, report: ~/.openclaw/wiki/main/reports/lint.md).
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The bridge import succeeded with 35 artifacts across 2 workspaces, `openclaw wiki status` reported bridge enabled with 35 exported artifacts, and wiki lint reported 0 issues.
- **Not tested**: I did not replace the live OpenClaw install with this source checkout. The live setup used the equivalent local repair shim; this PR moves that behavior into upstream source. URI-backed `memory-lancedb` behavior and discovery-only memory capability behavior are covered by local regression tests listed below.

target.title: fix(plugins): retain plugin tool registry after replacement
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: cached descriptor-backed plugin tools can lose their runtime registry after the active registry is replaced, then fail with `plugin tool runtime missing`.
- Why it matters: #80847 reports contracted plugin tools becoming unavailable in sub-agent sessions after a few successful calls, with current-main confirmation of this resolver path.
- What changed: when a forced cold tool-discovery load finds the scoped plugin tools, keep that registry active so later cached descriptor executions reuse the live runtime instead of falling back to an incomplete replacement registry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not broaden plugin allowlists, tool availability, or normal plugin loading; it only retains a successful scoped tool-discovery registry.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80847
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: cached plugin tool descriptors continue executing after active runtime registry replacement instead of failing with `plugin tool runtime missing`.
- Real environment tested: Linux x86_64 dev executor, Node v22.22.2, local OpenClaw runtime harness with a real config-path plugin loaded through `resolvePluginTools()`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx --input-type=module` local runtime harness: create a config-path plugin with `contracts.tools`, resolve/capture cached plugin descriptors, replace the active registry with an incomplete registry for the same plugin id plus an unrelated live tool, then execute the cached descriptor twice.
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts --testNamePattern 'retains cold-loaded plugin tools'`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/plugins/active-runtime-registry.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-pin.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw runtime harness shows the cached descriptor executing twice after incomplete active registry replacement while unrelated active registry tools survive; targeted regression tests, related plugin registry tests, and `pnpm check:changed` also passed locally.

  Live terminal output from a local OpenClaw runtime harness after this patch:

  ```text
  openclaw #80847 local runtime proof
  node=v22.22.2 repo=openclaw plugin=cache-live-proof tool=cache_live_tool
  plugin_file=file:///tmp/openclaw-80847-live-DR2not/plugin/index.cjs
  resolved_initial_tools=cache_live_tool
  resolved_cached_tools=cache_live_tool
  active_registry_after_replacement_tools=1
  active_registry_after_replacement_owners=unrelated-live
  execute_after_replacement_1=live-runtime-ok-1
  active_registry_after_first_execute_tools=1
  active_registry_after_first_execute_owners=unrelated-live
  execute_after_replacement_2=live-runtime-ok-2
  active_registry_after_second_execute_tools=1
  active_registry_after_second_execute_owners=unrelated-live
  result=PASS cached descriptor survived incomplete active registry replacement without replacing unrelated active registry tools
  ```
- Observed result after fix: first cached descriptor execution returned `live-runtime-ok-1`; the unrelated active registry owner remained `unrelated-live` after both executions; the second execution returned `live-runtime-ok-2` using the retained scoped recovery registry without replacing global active state.
- What was not tested: the reporter's private `lossless-claw` plugin and live five-minute sub-agent archival loop were not available here. Crabbox CLI was installed locally, but remote AWS Crabbox proof was blocked by missing local Crabbox/AWS auth (`broker=- auth=missing`; `crabbox doctor` failed at AWS credentials), so no remote Crabbox run is claimed.
- Before evidence: the new regression failed before the fix with `plugin tool runtime unavailable (cache-lifecycle-test): cached_lifecycle_tool` on the second cached descriptor execution.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `resolvePluginToolRegistry()` forced a cold standalone load when an active/channel registry existed but did not cover the requested plugin tools, but that successful cold-loaded registry was not retained for later cached descriptor executions.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing cached descriptor tests did not simulate active registry replacement followed by repeated cached tool execution.
- Contributing context (if known): recent registry compatibility hardening made partial registry reuse stricter, which is correct, but the recovery cold-load path was ephemeral.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: cached plugin tool descriptors execute successfully after the active registry is replaced by an incomplete registry for the same plugin id.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it directly exercises the resolver/cached descriptor path that throws the reported error without needing the reporter's private plugin.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Contracted plugin tools remain available across registry replacement/sub-agent lifecycle changes instead of intermittently failing until gateway restart.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
cached descriptor -> incomplete active registry -> one-off cold load -> later execution fails

After:
cached descriptor -> incomplete active registry -> scoped cold load retained -> later execution succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64
- Runtime/container: local Node/Vitest checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin tool runtime
- Relevant config (redacted): default test plugin config

### Steps

1. Resolve plugin tools once to seed cached descriptors.
2. Replace the active registry with an incomplete registry that still declares the same plugin id.
3. Execute the cached descriptor tool twice.

### Expected

- Both executions succeed; the first recovery cold-load is retained for the second execution.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the second execution rejected with `plugin tool runtime unavailable`.
- After this patch, both executions resolve successfully.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: local OpenClaw runtime harness with a real config-path plugin, RED/GREEN regression, related plugin registry tests, formatting, staged secret scan, changed gate.
- Edge cases checked: compatible active registry reuse, pinned channel registry behavior, active runtime registry filtering.
- What you did **not** verify: private reporter plugin, live sub-agent archival timing, remote Crabbox proof due local auth blocker.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: retaining a recovery registry could accidentally replace broader active/channel/http runtime state.
  - Mitigation: retention is descriptor-scoped and stored outside global active state; the regression now verifies unrelated active registry tools survive recovery execution.

target.title: Migrate auth credentials from Codex and Hermes
target.body:
## Summary

- Add interactive `openclaw migrate` credential prompting with yes selected by default, while keeping non-interactive `--yes` credential import explicit via `--include-secrets` and adding `--no-auth-credentials`.
- Make `--no-auth-credentials` authoritative when combined with `--include-secrets`, including direct `migrate plan` previews, so an explicit credential opt-out cannot import or preview sensitive auth material as planned.
- Migrate supported Hermes and Codex OAuth/API-key credentials into OpenClaw auth profiles and config, including OpenCode auth-store credentials for OpenAI OAuth, OpenCode, OpenCode Go, and non-enterprise GitHub Copilot.
- Discover OpenCode auth beside the migrated Hermes home and via applicable `XDG_DATA_HOME`, while skipping unsupported surfaces such as web-search-only Perplexity model auth and OpenCode Copilot enterprise routing.
- Add conflict, TOCTOU, and credential-fingerprint guards so a changed source auth file cannot write the wrong OAuth account during apply.
- Document and lock the public `applyProviderAuthConfigPatch` provider-auth SDK export used by migration providers.
- Repair current CI blockers by classifying/documenting the `meeting-notes` SDK subpath, moving meeting-notes tests onto public SDK imports, aligning the agent command runtime-plugin load expectation with current behavior, moving Discord's meeting-notes source registration behind a top-level API bridge, and stabilizing the non-isolated core fast plugin-boundary tests.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/migrate.test.ts extensions/codex/src/migration/provider.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/secrets.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/provider.secret-failure.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/migrate.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/configured-state.test.ts src/plugin-activation-boundary.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=core-unit-fast OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/migrate.ts src/commands/migrate.test.ts src/plugin-activation-boundary.test.ts src/channels/plugins/package-state-probes.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/migrate.ts src/commands/migrate.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/migrate-hermes/secrets.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/provider.secret-failure.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/migration/provider.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/secrets.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/provider.secret-failure.test.ts extensions/migrate-hermes/files-and-skills.test.ts src/commands/migrate.test.ts src/commands/migrate/output.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/meeting-notes/index.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts src/commands/agent.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/bundled.shape-guard.test.ts`
- `pnpm run lint:plugins:no-extension-test-core-imports`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs`
- `git diff --check`
- CI failed shard reproduced locally after the latest patch with the same include list and worker budget: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=<tmp> OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_VITEST_SHARD_NAME=agentic-control-plane-runtime OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts` (34 files, 302 tests passed).
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm build`
- Synthetic Hermes/OpenCode credential CLI smoke after rebuild: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state node openclaw.mjs migrate hermes --from <tmp>/.hermes --include-secrets --yes --no-backup --force --json`
- Interactive prompt smoke after rebuild: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state node openclaw.mjs migrate hermes --from <tmp>/.hermes --dry-run`, then pressed Enter on the default selected auth prompt.
- Synthetic Codex credential CLI smoke after rebuild: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state node openclaw.mjs migrate codex --from <tmp>/.codex --include-secrets --yes --no-backup --force --json`
- Synthetic auth opt-out apply smoke after rebuild: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state node openclaw.mjs migrate hermes --from <tmp>/.hermes --include-secrets --no-auth-credentials --yes --no-backup --force --json`
- Synthetic auth opt-out plan smoke after rebuild: `OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp>/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<tmp>/state node openclaw.mjs migrate plan hermes --from <tmp>/.hermes --include-secrets --no-auth-credentials --json`
- Autoreview note: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` was attempted twice after the migration fixes, once after the CI-blocker fix, and once after the auth opt-out fix. The Codex-engine runs wedged silently in the Python helper before producing reviewer output or spawning a `codex exec` subprocess. `--no-tools` is not supported by the Codex engine, so there is no clean autoreview result for this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw migrate` can import supported auth credentials from Hermes/OpenCode and Codex into OpenClaw auth profiles/config when credential migration is selected, prompts interactively with Yes selected by default, and honors an explicit `--no-auth-credentials` opt-out even if `--include-secrets` is also present. The opt-out now applies to both apply/default flows and direct `migrate plan` previews.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI from this branch after `pnpm build`, using disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME`/`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and synthetic Hermes/OpenCode/Codex auth stores with fake credential values only.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created disposable auth stores under `<tmp>` with synthetic OpenAI OAuth, OpenCode, OpenCode Go, GitHub Copilot, and Codex credentials. Ran the five CLI smoke commands listed in Verification, then inspected generated auth profile stores and plan output with secrets redacted to booleans.
Evidence after fix: Synthetic Hermes/OpenCode credential import finished with zero errors and wrote all four supported auth profiles, with secret values not printed:

```json
{
  "summary": {
    "total": 4,
    "planned": 0,
    "migrated": 4,
    "skipped": 0,
    "conflicts": 0,
    "errors": 0,
    "sensitive": 4
  },
  "migrated": [
    "auth:openai-codex",
    "secret:opencode:opencode-auth-json",
    "secret:opencode-go:opencode-auth-json",
    "secret:github-copilot:opencode-auth-json"
  ],
  "profiles": {
    "openai-codex:account-acct_synthetic": {
      "type": "oauth",
      "provider": "openai-codex",
      "hasAccess": true,
      "hasRefresh": true,
      "accountId": "acct_synthetic"
    },
    "opencode:hermes-import": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "opencode",
      "hasKey": true
    },
    "opencode-go:hermes-import": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "opencode-go",
      "hasKey": true
    },
    "github-copilot:github": {
      "type": "token",
      "provider": "github-copilot",
      "hasToken": true
    }
  }
}
```

Interactive prompt proof, with Enter accepting the default highlighted Yes selection:

```text
Do you want to migrate your auth credentials as well?
● Yes / ○ No

Do you want to migrate your auth credentials as well?
Yes

Migration preview: hermes
1 item, 0 conflicts, 1 sensitive item

Auth credentials:
• openai-codex [sensitive] (Import Hermes OpenAI Codex OAuth credentials and configure OpenAI Codex models.)
```

Synthetic Codex credential import migrated the Codex OAuth auth profile with zero errors, with token values redacted to booleans:

```json
{
  "summary": {
    "total": 1,
    "planned": 0,
    "migrated": 1,
    "skipped": 0,
    "conflicts": 0,
    "errors": 0,
    "sensitive": 1
  },
  "migrated": ["auth:openai-codex"],
  "profile": {
    "type": "oauth",
    "provider": "openai-codex",
    "hasAccess": true,
    "hasRefresh": true,
    "accountId": "acct_cli_codex"
  }
}
```

`--no-auth-credentials` apply precedence proof, with both `--include-secrets` and `--no-auth-credentials` present:

```json
{
  "summary": {
    "total": 1,
    "planned": 0,
    "migrated": 0,
    "skipped": 1,
    "conflicts": 0,
    "errors": 0,
    "sensitive": 1
  },
  "item": {
    "id": "auth:openai-codex",
    "kind": "auth",
    "action": "skip",
    "status": "skipped",
    "sensitive": true,
    "reason": "auth credential migration not selected",
    "details": {
      "provider": "openai-codex",
      "profileId": "openai-codex:account-acct_optout",
      "sourceCredentialIndex": "[redacted]",
      "sourceCredentialFingerprint": "[redacted]",
      "sourceProfileId": "openai-codex:account-acct_optout",
      "sourceKind": "hermes-auth-json",
      "sourceLabel": "Hermes active OpenAI Codex provider"
    }
  },
  "authStoreExists": false,
  "authStoreProfileCount": 0
}
```

`--no-auth-credentials` plan precedence proof, with both `--include-secrets` and `--no-auth-credentials` present:

```json
{
  "summary": {
    "total": 1,
    "planned": 0,
    "migrated": 0,
    "skipped": 1,
    "conflicts": 0,
    "errors": 0,
    "sensitive": 1
  },
  "item": {
    "id": "auth:openai-codex",
    "kind": "auth",
    "action": "skip",
    "status": "skipped",
    "sensitive": true,
    "reason": "auth credential migration not selected",
    "details": {
      "provider": "openai-codex",

```

Observed result after fix: Credential import works for the supported synthetic Hermes/OpenCode and Codex paths, the interactive prompt defaults to Yes and imports when accepted, and the explicit auth opt-out path skips the detected sensitive OAuth item in both apply and plan flows.
What was not tested: Live third-party OAuth refresh against real external accounts, OpenCode Copilot enterprise endpoint routing, and broad remote/Testbox lanes. Maintainers should still deliberately approve the yes-default credential prompt and the public provider-auth SDK export as UX/API-contract choices.

target.title: fix(doctor): warn and continue when cron store is unreadable
target.body:
## Summary
- warn and continue in `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` when the cron store exists but cannot be read because of permission/access errors
- keep non-permission cron store failures strict so malformed JSON and other corruption still surface
- add focused regression tests for both paths

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` should not stop early when the legacy cron store exists but is unreadable because of permission errors; malformed cron store content should still remain a hard failure.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with a real temporary `cron/jobs.json` file, real filesystem permission changes (`chmod 000`), and direct execution of the patched doctor cron path via `node --import tsx`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- Create a temporary cron store file containing one persisted job, change the file mode to `000`, and run `maybeRepairLegacyCronStore` through `node --import tsx`.
- Create a separate temporary cron store file containing malformed JSON and run the same path again.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Doctor warnings
Cron store at /media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json could not be read.
Doctor skipped legacy cron store checks and will continue with later health checks.
Read error: EACCES: permission denied, open '/media/vdc/0668001470/tmp/openclaw-doctor-cron-ZT95Ot/cron/jobs.json'
UNREADABLE_OK
PARSE_THROW_OK
```

Observed result after fix: the unreadable cron store path emitted a `Doctor warnings` note and returned without throwing, which preserves later doctor health checks; the malformed cron store path still threw the parse failure and the verification script reported `PARSE_THROW_OK`.

What was not tested: full `openclaw doctor` end-to-end execution across every later health contribution, and non-Linux permission behavior such as Windows or macOS ACL differences.

## Testing
- `git diff --check`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, JSON.stringify({ version: 1, jobs: [{ id: "job-1", name: "Job 1", enabled: true, createdAtMs: Date.now(), updatedAtMs: Date.now(), schedule: { kind: "every", everyMs: 60000 }, payload: { kind: "systemEvent", text: "hi" }, state: {} }] }, null, 2), "utf8"); await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o000); try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); console.log("UNREADABLE_OK"); } finally { await fs.chmod(storePath, 0o600).catch(() => undefined); await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); }'`
- `node --import tsx --eval 'import fs from "node:fs/promises"; import os from "node:os"; import path from "node:path"; import { maybeRepairLegacyCronStore } from "./src/commands/doctor-cron.ts"; const root = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), "openclaw-doctor-cron-parse-")); const storePath = path.join(root, "cron", "jobs.json"); await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(storePath), { recursive: true }); await fs.writeFile(storePath, "{ not json", "utf8"); let threw = false; try { await maybeRepairLegacyCronStore({ cfg: { cron: { store: storePath, webhook: "https://example.invalid/cron" } }, options: {}, prompter: { confirm: async () => true } }); } catch (err) { threw = String(err).includes("Failed to parse cron store"); if (threw) { console.log("PARSE_THROW_OK"); } else { console.error(String(err)); process.exitCode = 1; } } finally { await fs.rm(root, { recursive: true, force: true }); } if (!threw) { console.error("PARSE_THROW_FAIL"); process.exitCode = 1; }'`

Closes #86102.

target.title: fix(cli): enforce local agent hard timeout
target.body:
## Problem

`openclaw agent --local --timeout N` can still leave the CLI alive until an outer wrapper timeout kills it. The lower-level embedded agent timeout is useful, but it is not a command-level wall-clock guarantee: setup, plugin registration, retries, cleanup, or a wedged embedded run can keep the CLI process around after the operator-specified timeout window.

## Change and value

This PR adds a CLI-level hard timeout for embedded local agent runs. Explicit `--local` runs and embedded fallback runs now share a wall-clock boundary of the resolved finite timeout plus 30 seconds of grace. If that timer fires, the CLI aborts the local run signal and exits with code `124`, matching the exit code operators already see from GNU `timeout` wrappers.

The value is that scripts no longer need to wait for their own much larger timeout window just because the embedded run failed to settle cleanly.

## Who is affected, and who is not

- Affected: operators and automation running `openclaw agent --local` with a finite `--timeout` or configured agent timeout.
- Affected: gateway-backed runs only after they enter embedded fallback; the normal Gateway request path keeps its existing timeout and `chat.abort` behavior.
- Not affected: `--timeout 0` or a configured local agent timeout of `0`; those continue to mean no CLI hard timeout.
- Not fixed here: post-success event-loop handles that keep the process alive after a successful local result. That is covered separately by #86264.

## Why now

Local diagnostics showed the no-completion CLI-exit class directly: an `agent --local` run can fail to produce a final result before the outer wrapper kills it, even when the inner timeout is shorter. This patch makes the local CLI boundary own that wall-clock guarantee instead of relying only on the embedded runner to unwind itself.

## Implementation

- Arms a startup local-agent hard timer before full plugin/command registration, so `agent --local --timeout N` is bounded even if setup hangs before the agent command action is reached.
- When `--timeout` is omitted, resolves the startup boundary from `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` before command/plugin registration instead of using an unconditional fallback; explicit `--timeout` still wins, and `--timeout 0` disables the timer.
- Keeps the lower-level embedded-run helper boundary around the existing `agentCommand` call sites.
- Uses the resolved finite timeout plus 30s as the hard deadline, with `0` disabling the timer.
- Forwards the resolved timeout into `agentCommand`, so cooperative embedded abort and the outer hard boundary agree on the same timeout.
- Preserves `SIGINT`/`SIGTERM` precedence: signals still exit promptly with `130`/`143`, even if the embedded run ignores abort or rejects during teardown.
- On timeout, aborts the signal, writes a concise timeout error, and exits through `runtime.exit(124)`.

## Compatibility and availability tradeoff

Today, some local agent commands can keep running long after their timeout has passed. That is especially painful for scripts, CI jobs, cron jobs, and agent dispatch tests, because the caller has to wait for a larger outer timeout or kill the process manually.

This PR changes that behavior for finite local-agent timeouts. When `openclaw agent --local` reaches the requested timeout plus 30 seconds of grace, OpenClaw exits the CLI with code `124` instead of continuing to wait for stuck background work.

That can change behavior for users who unintentionally relied on the old lingering process. For example, a script that used to keep waiting after `--timeout 60` may now see OpenClaw exit after about 90 seconds. This is intentional: once the timeout and grace window have passed, the safer default is to release the caller rather than let one stuck local agent hold the whole workflow indefinitely.

Users who intentionally want unbounded local-agent runs can keep using `--timeout 0`, or configure the local agent timeout as `0`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw agent --local --timeout N` and embedded fallback runs should have a command-level wall-clock timeout even if setup or the local run fails to settle after lower-level aborts.

Real environment tested: Linux/WSL local OpenClaw setup. Before baseline was global OpenClaw `2026.5.20 (e510042)` with the local `cli-exit-fix` patch still applied. Current PR head is `6befa4a1a0c1157930fa632f921d9092a4ea0fb4`. The original live timeout proof was captured on #86276 head `e0e10b389dc3c08b27890db5d78e47d3fc9ef58f`; the follow-up configured-timeout correction on `6befa4a1a0c1157930fa632f921d9092a4ea0fb4` is covered by the focused regression tests listed in Verification. Combined stack proof used #86276 `e0e10b389dc3c08b27890db5d78e47d3fc9ef58f` plus #86264 `98802c0f63c0a4fcc8639b8dde535f3bed879207`, combined head `6a806f8f19628c16f99627162b8f53a01e27e680`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
timeout 70s node /tmp/openclaw-combined-package/openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent eval-1 --timeout 1 --model ollama-cloud/glm-5.1 -m 'Reply with exactly the word: PONG' --json
timeout 300s node /tmp/openclaw-cli-exit-combined/openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent eval-1 --timeout 180 --model ollama-cloud/glm-5.1 -m 'Reply with exactly the word: PONG' --json
timeout 240s node /tmp/openclaw-cli-exit-combined/openclaw.mjs agent --local --agent main --session-id combined-cli-exit-proof --message 'Return marker OPENCLAW_CLI_EXIT_COMBINED_PROOF_OK' --thinking off --json
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal transcripts and copied console output were captured under `/home/captain/codex-workspace/proof/2026-05-25-cli-exit-combined/`, including `25-after-combined-package-early-boundary-timeout1.out`, `31-after-combined-source-success-pong.out`, and `32-after-combined-source-mock-success.out`.

```text
local agent command timed out after 1s plus 30s grace
EXIT_CODE=124
ELAPSED_SEC=39

[agent/embedded] [trace:embedded-run] ... phase=attempt-dispatch ...
local agent command timed out after 180s plus 30s grace
EXIT_CODE=124
ELAPSED_SEC=212

finalAssistantVisibleText: OPENCLAW_CLI_EXIT_COMBINED_PROOF_OK
executionTrace.runner: embedded
executionTrace.attempts[0].result: success
mock request path: /v1/responses
EXIT_CODE=0
ELAPSED_SEC=41
ASSERT_AGENT_TURN=passed
```

Observed result after fix: The `--timeout 1` local-agent command exited from OpenClaw's own hard boundary with code `124` after the configured timeout plus grace, instead of falling through to the outer wrapper. A longer live local-agent run also exited from OpenClaw's own `180s + 30s` boundary. The combined success-path run produced a final embedded-agent result and exited normally with code `0`.

Before evidence: On the production baseline, the local workaround was still firing instead of upstream owning the timeout.

```bash
timeout 70s openclaw agent --local --agent eval-1 --timeout 1 --model ollama-cloud/glm-5.1 -m 'Reply with exactly the word: PONG' --json
```

```text
[openclaw] cli-exit-fix: hard wall-clock SIGKILL after 31s (timeout=1s + 30s grace)
EXIT_CODE=1
ELAPSED_SEC=40
```

Before evidence optional: During review, old draft #86276 head `8790ec43051f30fb48a5b8772ff4f3671f9307cd2` stacked with #86264 still exited only from an outer `timeout 70s` wrapper and did not print OpenClaw's timeout message. A plugin trace showed repeated pre-agent discovery/registration work, proving the first draft armed the boundary too late.

What was not tested: a standalone package install from this exact branch without #86264. The success-path exit proof above is intentionally run on the combined stack because #86264 owns the post-completion cleanup class.

## Verification

Standalone #86276 head `6befa4a1a0c1157930fa632f921d9092a4ea0fb4`:

- `env OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 timeout 600s node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/cli/run-main.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (41/41; covers configured omitted-timeout resolver, explicit override precedence, and configured `0` disabling)
- `env OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 timeout 600s node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (90/90; covers omitted `--timeout` plus configured timeout before command registration)
- `env OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_SERIAL=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 timeout 600s node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (54/54)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/cli/run-main.ts src/cli/run-main.test.ts src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

Combined stack `6a806f8f19628c16f99627162b8f53a01e27e680`:

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm test:unit:fast -- src/cli/run-main.test.ts src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/cli/run-main.ts src/cli/run-main.test.ts src/cli/run-main.exit.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/context-engine-maintenance.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Relationship to #86264

#86264 handles the post-completion cleanup class: a command has finished but deferred context-engine maintenance keeps work alive. This PR handles the no-completion class: a local/embedded agent command never settles, so the CLI needs a hard wall-clock boundary. The combined mock-agent proof verifies the two fixes compose: successful local results still exit normally, while wedged/no-completion runs are capped by this PR.

AI-assisted: yes.

target.title: feat(signal): support reaction approvals
target.body:
Summary

- Add Signal native exec/plugin approval capability gated by top-level approvals configuration.
- Add thumbs-up / thumbs-down reaction approval handling for allow-once and deny, with explicit Signal approver auth.
- Bind reactions only from native approval delivery, verify target-author identity, persist binding state, and re-check current approval route config before resolving.
- Harden reaction bindings by normalizing Signal UUID author keys and removing timestamp-only cross-conversation fallback.

Verification

- [x] node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-native.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
- [x] pnpm check:changed
- [x] git diff --check
- [x] Manual dev.review loop passes addressed target route gating, target-author verification, generic send trust boundary, reaction hint binding, persisted route re-checks, and exact reaction binding scope.

Behavior addressed: Signal can deliver approval prompts through native approval delivery and resolve authorized 👍 / 👎 reactions as allow-once / deny without cross-conversation timestamp fallback.
Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/signal/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-native.test.ts extensions/signal/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts; pnpm check:changed; git diff --check
Evidence after fix: focused Vitest passed 3 files / 24 tests; pnpm check:changed passed extension and extension-test lanes.
Observed result after fix: Signal UUID author-key casing matches, reaction binding stays exact to the conversation/message key, and changed-lane typecheck/lint checks pass.
What was not tested: live Signal transport/reaction roundtrip against a real Signal account in this fix pass.
